text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY SURVIVORSHIP FOLLOW-UP NOTE

PATIENT: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
VISIT TYPE: Surveillance Follow-Up — 24-Month Post-Treatment Milestone
ATTENDING: Head and Neck Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Ms. Osei-Bonsu is a 54-year-old West African-descent female, never-smoker, with a history of p16-positive (HPV-associated) squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition stage III conventional staging; HPV-positive oropharyngeal stage I), treated with definitive concurrent chemoradiation completed approximately 24 months ago. She presents today for her 24-month surveillance visit. She had a PET-CT performed prior to today's appointment, the results of which are reviewed here.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Ms. Osei-Bonsu initially presented at age 52 with a 3-month history of right-sided odynophagia and a palpable right neck mass. She was diagnosed with HPV-positive (p16 strongly positive, HPV ISH positive for high-risk type 16) moderately differentiated squamous cell carcinoma of the right base of tongue with a single ipsilateral right level IIA lymph node measuring 2.1 cm. Staging workup including MRI neck and PET-CT confirmed T2N1M0 disease without distant spread. NGS from tumor biopsy demonstrated a PIK3CA E545K activating mutation, TERT promoter mutation, FAT1 nonsense mutation, CCND1 amplification, CDKN2A deletion, and low-level EGFR copy number gain; MSS; TMB low at 6.2 mut/Mb; PD-L1 CPS 12. The absence of TP53 mutation was consistent with HPV-driven oncogenesis.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, she was treated with definitive IMRT (70 Gy to high-risk CTV in 35 fractions via SIB-VMAT technique) with concurrent weekly cisplatin at 40 mg/m2. She received 6 of 7 planned cycles (cumulative dose 414 mg/m2) — one cycle held in week 5 due to grade 2 nephrotoxicity and grade 2 thrombocytopenia. Treatment was complicated by grade 3 oral mucositis in week 4, requiring a brief hospitalization for IV hydration and pain management, during which PEG tube feeds were initiated. She also developed grade 2 radiation dermatitis, grade 2 dysgeusia, grade 2 xerostomia, and grade 2 nephrotoxicity (creatinine returning to baseline with hydration). No significant ototoxicity or peripheral neuropathy occurred during treatment.

Post-treatment restaging at 10 weeks showed a complete metabolic response on PET-CT (no FDG-avid disease, formerly avid right level IIA node now a small fibrotic remnant). MRI corroborated this with a residual 0.9 cm fibrotic-appearing area at the right base of tongue, prompting confirmatory direct laryngoscopy and biopsy at approximately 5 months post-treatment. Pathology from all sampled sites was negative for viable carcinoma, confirming a complete pathologic response.

She underwent PEG tube removal at approximately 7 months post-treatment after achieving adequate oral caloric intake. Her 9-month surveillance PET-CT was negative. At the 12-month mark she had returned to her profession as a high school music teacher and was doing well clinically. MRI neck at 18 months was stable, showing continued regression of the residual fibrotic area to 0.5 cm and no new adenopathy.

Today she reaches the 24-month post-treatment milestone. She reports no new symptoms. She denies any new oral or neck pain, any new lumps or bumps in the neck, difficulty breathing or stridor, hemoptysis, odynophagia, or otalgia. She does acknowledge persistent dry mouth, which she manages with frequent water intake, Biotene gel, and pilocarpine. She has no new headaches, diplopia, or changes in cranial nerve function. She denies new constitutional symptoms such as fever, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. She is eating well and has maintained stable weight. She is active professionally and personally.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

General: No fatigue. No unintentional weight loss. No fever or chills.
HEENT: Persistent grade 1 dry mouth, stable. No new oral pain. No new neck swelling. No otalgia. No change in voice quality (mild stable hoarseness, unchanged). No epistaxis. No trismus symptoms at present.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or dyspnea. No known carotid symptoms (no TIA, no amaurosis fugax).
Respiratory: No shortness of breath. No cough. No hemoptysis.
Gastrointestinal: Good appetite. Tolerating varied diet. No dysphagia with regular-texture foods; some avoidance of very dry or sticky foods (crackers, peanut butter) but otherwise no significant limitation. No nausea/vomiting.
Genitourinary: No dysuria or hematuria. No new renal symptoms.
Musculoskeletal: No arthralgias.
Neurological: No new deficits. No neuropathy. Hearing: mild high-frequency change noted previously, stable, not causing functional difficulty.
Endocrine: Stable on levothyroxine, no hypothyroid symptoms.
Psychosocial: Doing well emotionally. Anxiety around today's scan results, which she describes as manageable. Active social support from partner and colleagues.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

Vital Signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 74 bpm, RR 14/min, SpO2 98% on room air, Temperature 36.7°C. Weight: 71 kg (stable over the past 6 months). BMI 24.1 kg/m2.

General: Well-appearing female in no acute distress. Appears her stated age. Well-nourished and healthy-looking.

Head: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No scalp lesions.

Eyes: PERRL. Extraocular movements intact. No proptosis. Sclera non-icteric.

Oral Cavity / Oropharynx: Mouth opens comfortably; estimated inter-incisal distance approximately 4.0-4.1 cm (improved from post-treatment nadir). Dentition intact, no oral mucosal lesions, no leukoplakia, no erythematous patches. Tongue: normal movement, no new masses palpable. Oropharynx: mucosa with residual post-radiation changes — slightly pale, somewhat atrophic appearance of the right base of tongue region. No visible mucosal irregularity, nodularity, or exophytic lesion. Salivary pooling appears reduced but not absent. Tonsils absent (right tonsillar fossa with small post-treatment fibrotic cuff, no mass). Uvula midline.

Neck: Supple. No palpable lymphadenopathy in levels I, II, III, IV, or V bilaterally. No palpable masses or fullness in the right neck. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Prior radiation field skin: mild hyperpigmentation with subcutaneous soft-tissue changes in the right neck, no evidence of active dermatitis. No lymphedema of the face or neck. Carotid pulses palpable and equal bilaterally, no bruits auscultated.

Thyroid: Non-tender, no palpable nodules, no goiter.

Flexible Nasopharyngoscopy (performed in clinic): Nasal passages: clear, no mucosal lesions. Nasopharynx: no mass. Oropharynx/base of tongue: the right base of tongue demonstrates expected post-radiation changes — pale, somewhat fibrotic mucosa without any raised, ulcerated, or nodular lesion. No neovascularization or abnormal mucosal pattern on white light examination. Hypopharynx: symmetric piriform sinuses, no pooling of secretions, no mass. Larynx: epiglottis unremarkable, bilateral vocal cord mobility intact, no supraglottic or glottic lesions. No evidence of lymphedema of the larynx or hypopharynx.

Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or rhonchi.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. PEG site: well-healed, no residual stigmata.

Extremities: No edema. No clubbing. No peripheral lymphadenopathy.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II through XII grossly intact. No tongue deviation. No shoulder weakness (CN XI preserved). No sensory deficits in the trigeminal distribution.

---

LAB RESULTS (reviewed today):

Complete Blood Count: WBC 6.2 x10^9/L, Hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL, Platelets 228 x10^9/L — all within normal limits.
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Creatinine 0.85 mg/dL (baseline recovered; eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m2), BUN 14 mg/dL, sodium 139, potassium 4.1, bicarbonate 25, glucose 94 mg/dL. Liver enzymes: AST 22, ALT 18, alkaline phosphatase 68, total bilirubin 0.6 — normal.
TSH: 1.6 mIU/L — euthyroid on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. (TSH has been well-controlled since dose adjustment approximately 18 months ago following radiation-induced hypothyroidism.)
Lipid Panel (repeat from 6 months ago on atorvastatin 10 mg): LDL 88 mg/dL (down from 118 mg/dL pre-statin), HDL 62 mg/dL, triglycerides 102 mg/dL — good response to low-dose statin therapy initiated for cardiovascular risk reduction in the setting of prior neck irradiation.
Magnesium: 1.9 mg/dL — low-normal, noted; patient advised to continue dietary magnesium supplementation. No IV repletion needed.

---

IMAGING RESULTS:

PET-CT (18F-FDG), 24-Month Surveillance:
Reviewed in detail. There is no FDG-avid activity at the right base of tongue — the SUVmax in this region is 1.7, which is well within the physiologic range and lower than what was seen even on prior surveillance scans (9-month: SUVmax 1.9; 18-month MRI: non-avid). The previously identified small residual fibrotic area at the right base of tongue has continued to decrease in conspicuity and shows no abnormal metabolic activity. The right level IIA nodal region shows a small stable fibrotic remnant without any FDG uptake. No pathologic adenopathy is identified anywhere in the neck — no new right or left cervical, retropharyngeal, or supraclavicular nodes with abnormal uptake. No mediastinal or hilar nodal disease. Thorax: lungs clear, no pulmonary nodules. Abdomen and pelvis: no hepatic or splenic lesions, no abdominal adenopathy. Bones: no focal FDG-avid osseous lesions. Thyroid: no FDG-avid thyroid nodules identified on this scan.

Overall impression from PET-CT: No evidence of disease — the scan looks the same as, if not cleaner than, the 9-month scan. This is exactly the expected appearance at 24 months in a complete responder to definitive CRT for HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. HPV-POSITIVE RIGHT BASE OF TONGUE SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMA (T2N1M0), STATUS POST DEFINITIVE CONCURRENT CHEMORADIATION — COMPLETE RESPONSE MAINTAINED AT 24 MONTHS:

Ms. Osei-Bonsu has achieved and maintained a complete response to definitive chemoradiation for her stage III (HPV-positive oropharyngeal stage I) base of tongue SCC. Today's PET-CT at the 24-month mark is entirely reassuring — no FDG-avid disease anywhere. This is a highly significant milestone. In the context of HPV-positive oropharyngeal SCC, the vast majority of recurrences, if they occur, happen within the first 24 months. The biology of HPV-driven OPSCC confers a much more favorable prognosis compared to HPV-negative disease, and sustained complete metabolic response at 2 years is strongly predictive of long-term cure. We estimate her 5-year locoregional control probability at approximately 85-90% and 5-year overall survival at approximately 90%, reflecting the combination of complete response, HPV-positive status, never-smoker history, and young age at diagnosis.

I counseled the patient extensively on the significance of this milestone. She was understandably relieved and emotional during this discussion. I reinforced that while we will continue surveillance, we are highly optimistic about her long-term outcome. Late recurrences beyond 24 months do occur — particularly distant metastases in HPV-positive OPSCC, which can occasionally manifest later than local recurrences — and she should continue to be vigilant about new symptoms. However, the risk decreases substantially at this point.

Surveillance plan going forward:
- Clinical exam: every 6 months (decreasing from current every 4-6 months schedule)
- Imaging: annual surveillance will be transitioned to clinical-exam-driven; we will not routinely scan annually at this point given her excellent status and the low incremental yield of annual PET-CT beyond 2 years in this setting. We will obtain imaging if clinical exam raises any concern or if new symptoms develop. This approach is consistent with NCCN guidelines for HPV-positive OPSCC in complete responders.
- Annual thyroid ultrasound will continue for radiation-induced thyroid cancer screening.
- Carotid artery Doppler: repeat in 12 months (next due at approximately 42 months post-radiation).

2. LATE RADIATION TOXICITIES — ONGOING MANAGEMENT:

Xerostomia (grade 1, stable): Patient continues pilocarpine 5 mg BID (tapered from TID at 18 months as noted in prior visit). She is managing well with Biotene gel and regular water intake. No significant change. We will continue current management. If symptoms worsen, consider increasing pilocarpine back to TID or evaluating for acupuncture as a complementary approach for xerostomia (evidence is modest but some patients report benefit).

Dysphagia (grade 1, improved): The patient now tolerates a regular diet with only minor modifications — she avoids extremely dry or sticky foods. This represents significant improvement from the post-treatment period when she required PEG tube feeding. No formal FEES or modified barium swallow is indicated at this time given her subjective and functional improvement. She will continue quarterly speech-language pathology check-ins, transitioning to twice-yearly going forward.

Trismus (resolved): Mouth opening now 4.0-4.1 cm, close to estimated baseline of 4.5 cm. Patient should continue TheraBite exercises as maintenance, though intensity can be reduced now that she has achieved near-normal range of motion.

Sensorineural hearing loss (grade 1, stable): Mild high-frequency SNHL at 4000-8000 Hz bilaterally on prior audiogram, attributed to cisplatin ototoxicity. Not causing functional difficulty. Annual audiologic assessment to monitor for progression.

Hypothyroidism (radiation-induced): Well-controlled on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. TSH 1.6 mIU/L today. Continue current dose. Annual thyroid function testing.

Cardiovascular risk — radiation-induced carotid artery changes: Low-dose atorvastatin 10 mg daily was initiated at the 30-month mark (approximately 6 months ago) for cardiovascular risk reduction. LDL now 88 mg/dL on statin, which is a good response. No carotid symptoms. Carotid Doppler scheduled at next annual visit. Lifestyle counseling reinforced (heart-healthy diet, physical activity).

3. GERMLINE BRCA2 VARIANT OF UNCERTAIN SIGNIFICANCE (p.D2723H) — GENETICS UPDATE:

As noted in the prior clinic note, genetics counseling reclassified the BRCA2 p.D2723H variant as likely benign based on updated population databases and functional evidence. No additional BRCA-directed screening or testing is recommended for the patient or her family members at this time. The patient was re-counseled today and is comfortable with this conclusion. She will continue age-appropriate routine cancer screening (mammography, cervical cytology as applicable).

4. HYPOTHYROIDISM:
See above — well-managed. Continue levothyroxine 100 mcg PO daily.

5. HEALTH MAINTENANCE / PREVENTIVE CARE:
- Dental hygiene: reinforced importance of meticulous oral hygiene and fluoride treatments to prevent dental caries in the setting of chronic xerostomia. She is followed by her dentist every 6 months with prescription fluoride rinse (1.1% NaF gel, nightly).
- Nutritional status: weight 71 kg, stable. BMI 24.1. Dietetics follow-up annually.
- HPV vaccination: patient is age 54; HPV vaccination is not indicated at this age per current guidelines. Partner counseling not applicable at this point.
- Return precautions: patient instructed to contact the clinic promptly for any new neck mass, new difficulty swallowing or talking, new ear pain, hemoptysis, new oral lesion, or unintentional weight loss.

TOTAL TIME IN CLINIC: 60 minutes, of which greater than 50% was spent in counseling and coordination of care.

ECOG PERFORMANCE STATUS: 0

CANCER STATUS: No evidence of disease. Complete response maintained at 24 months post-definitive chemoradiation.

PLAN FOR NEXT VISIT: In approximately 6 months. Patient will call with any interim concerns. She is aware of the surveillance schedule and return precautions.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Head and Neck Medical Oncology
